comments.text: The provenance of the claim that this conjecture implies $p_{n+1} - p_n < p_n^{1/2-c}$ is a little murky. Theorem 1 of the 1971 paper of Erdos and Selfridge gets the weaker bound $p_{n+1} -p_n \ll p_n^{1/2} / \log^{1/2} p_n$ (which is already enough to get Legendre's conjecture asymptotically) and assert without proof that the result of Ramachandra concerning the existence of non-smooth numbers in short intervals gets the power saving, but on page 15 they write "we do not give the details here". Theorem 1 is proven by noting that if Grimm's conjecture held, then $\binom{n+k}{k}$ would have to be divisible by at least $k-\pi(k)$ primes larger than $k$, and this and standard prime number theorem and Stirling approximation estimates is enough to conclude. I guess Ramachandra's result would allow one to say that some of these primes are actually rather large, but the actual calculations seem to require some care (one needs a "positive density" version of Ramachandra's result, I think).
More generally, Langevin conjectured that any sequence of consecutive numbers, all of which are composite, must form a set of multiplicatively independent numbers.

In Grimm's conjecture on consecutive integers(2006), S. Laishram and T. N. Shorey have proved this conjecture for $n \leq 1.9 \times 10^{10}$ and for all k. P. S. I believe the bound by [RST75] can be pushed further (under stronger conjectures like Elliot Halberstam Conjecture). Edit: Earlier, there was an embarrassing careless mistake here. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Note; the cubic boune of RST75 is stronger than the quadratic bound. By Cramer s conjecture, the bound of RST75 and the verification for small n, the answer to this problem should surely be positive.
